title: Dang it, Doug.
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman rebukes Doug Wilson for suggesting abolitionists owe pro-life incrementalists thanks if Roe v. Wade is overturned in Dobbs, arguing instead that 50 years of incrementalist obsequiousness toward the Supreme Court has enabled the court's tyranny and that any reversal will be due to abolitionist defiance and nullification efforts.
audiences: Doug Wilson sympathizers and Reformed evangelicals

questions.question: What specific claim by Doug Wilson prompted this rebuke from Free the States?
questions.audience: Doug Wilson sympathizers and Reformed evangelicals
questions.answer: On a February 2nd, 2022 episode of his podcast, Wilson argued that if the Dobbs decision overturns Roe and returns abortion regulation to the states, abolitionists ought to gratefully thank the pro-life incrementalists they have long criticized for handing them the opportunity to act at the state level.
questions.quote: If the Dobbs case goes the way it ought to, and the decision whether to restrict abortion is handed back to the states, it will have been the incrementalists who gave the abolitionists on the state level their opportunity, and I think it’d be nice if the abolitionists were gracious enough to say ‘thank you.’

questions.question: What is the empirical track record of pro-life incrementalist litigation at the Supreme Court since 1973?
questions.answer: Silberman points out that 38 abortion-related cases have reached the Supreme Court since Roe and not one has produced any rollback of the decision; Casey in 1992 actually strengthened Roe with an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority, with the lone Democrat being the pro-life dissenter Byron White.
questions.quote: Since Roe was decided in 1973, 38 abortion-related cases have come before the supreme court. Not a single one of those cases has resulted in even the tiniest rollback of the Roe decision.

questions.question: How does the article characterize the posture of pro-life leaders toward the Supreme Court?
questions.answer: It calls their posture obsequious and idolatrous, citing Texas Alliance for Life director Joe Pojman comparing the court's authority to gravity and Scott Klusendorf describing the court's pronouncements as having been dictated 'from on high'—language Silberman treats as functional worship of the justices.
questions.quote: Saying that the supreme court is “on high” and that their authority is akin to the laws of nature are two of the most blatantly idolatrous statements about the court from pro-life leaders, but nearly every pro-life organization believes similarly.

questions.question: Why, according to Silberman, do the justices feel free to keep upholding Roe?
questions.answer: Because pro-life leaders have signaled across decades that there is no act of judicial tyranny they will resist, the justices know they face no real consequence for rubber-stamping abortion and therefore have no incentive to repent.
questions.quote: They know they can rubber stamp genocide as many times as they want and the pro-life leaders will never stand up to them. They know there is no line they can cross that would cause the pro-life leaders to bow up.

questions.question: What concrete progress has the abolitionist movement made by 2022 that the article credits for pressuring the court?
questions.answer: Bills to nullify Roe and abolish abortion have been filed in 15 states, abolitionist challengers are unseating incrementalist incumbents, abolition bills have picked up as many as 10 coauthors in some states, and the Southern Baptist Convention has passed a resolution affirming a duty of lower civil authorities to nullify unjust Supreme Court rulings.
questions.quote: Bills to nullify Roe and abolish abortion have been filed in 15 states. Abolitionist challengers are unseating powerful incrementalist incumbents each election cycle. Abolition bills are picking up steam in state legislatures, and have gotten as many as 10 coauthors in some states.

questions.question: What did the abolitionist amicus brief in Dobbs communicate to the Supreme Court?
questions.answer: The 48-page brief authored by Bradley Pierce, which Free the States signed onto, warned the court that states intend to abolish abortion in defiance of Roe regardless of how Dobbs is decided unless the court itself repents.
questions.quote: Pierce explained to the court that the states are going to abolish abortion in defiance of Roe unless the court repents and kisses the Son.

questions.question: What does Silberman predict the court is most likely to actually do in Dobbs?
questions.answer: He predicts the likeliest outcome is a 15-week compromise rather than a full reversal of Roe, citing Roberts's openly stated preference at oral argument and the unreliability of Kavanaugh and Gorsuch on social issues.
questions.quote: Chief Justice John Roberts openly signaled his preference for a 15-week compromise during oral arguments, and Brett Kavanaugh and Neil Gorsuch have been unreliable on social issues thus far. The 15-week compromise appears to be the likeliest outcome

questions.question: What is the article's closing prescription for Christians who care about abolition?
questions.audience: Doug Wilson sympathizers and Reformed evangelicals
questions.answer: Both for obedience to Christ and for tactical pressure on the justices, Christians should rally around abolition bills and stop entertaining the incrementalist arguments coming from Wilson, The Gospel Coalition, and similar voices.
questions.quote: Christians must rally around abolition bills and have nothing more to do with the ignorant, incrementalist nonsense being peddled by Doug Wilson, The Gospel Coalition, et al.

related.title: No Quarter November: How Is Doug Wilson Not an Abolitionist?
related.reason: Silberman's earlier extended critique of Wilson, explicitly referenced and linked in the opening paragraph of this article.

related.title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
related.reason: Directly linked in the article as the source of the 15-week compromise prediction discussed near the end.

related.title: Refuting Toby Sumpter's "Eight Tenets of Smashmouth Incrementalism"
related.reason: Continues the same Free the States critique of the Wilson/Canon Press camp's incrementalist framing.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Companion piece published days later attacking the theological premises that underwrite pro-life incrementalism.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Develops the same charge of pro-life judicial idolatry that this article levels against Pojman, Klusendorf, and Wilson.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Earlier Silberman piece dismantling another high-profile defender of incrementalism, useful as background for the broader argument.

related.title: Robert L. Dabney: Wrong on Slavery, Right on Abolitionism
related.reason: Tagged with Doug Wilson and Smashmouth Incrementalism, providing the historical-theology context for the abolitionism-vs-incrementalism debate.
